name: B.S. in Architecture and M.S. in Civil Engineering

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: Unlike the other combined B.S. degrees, the full eight-term plan parsed, and it is well ordered: the studio ladder ARCH 195 -> 196 -> 295 -> 296 -> 395 runs term over term (deepestPrereqChain 4) with History I->II and Construction I->II sequenced alongside, PHYS 102/102A landing in Second Year, and Years 3-4 opening into architecture electives. The one coherence concern is engineering readiness: for a program feeding an M.S. in Civil Engineering, the visible math is only MATH 107 (University Mathematics A) and MATH 105 (statistics), with no calculus, statics, or structures in the undergraduate spine.
dimensions.suggestion: Insert a calculus sequence and an introductory statics/structures course into the undergraduate terms so students enter the M.S. Civil Engineering with the expected engineering-mechanics foundation.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is clean and appropriately shallow (deepestPrereqChain 4; e.g., ARCH 296 <- ARCH 295, ARCH 395 <- ARCH 224 and a studio/history course). missingCourseCount is only 9, and the danglers are all graduate or placeholder items: ARCH 601/602/636/651/677/684 (graduate civil/architecture courses), GER 200/300 categories, and the PHYS 102A lab co-requisite. No undergraduate chain is broken.
dimensions.suggestion: Register the PHYS 102A lab and the GER 200/300 category codes, and link the graduate ARCH 6xx courses, so missingCourseCount drops toward zero.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives appear in the data despite the otherwise-complete plan. A B.S. Architecture + M.S. Civil Engineering especially needs an explicit statement of the integrated design-plus-structural-engineering competency that distinguishes it.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 5-8 program learning outcomes covering architectural design, building technology, and the structural/civil-engineering competencies the M.S. adds.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: This program reconciles exactly: creditReconcileDelta is 0.0 with summedTermCredits 120 equal to the stated 120. The visible distribution is reasonable, blending the architecture core (studios, history, construction), a science base (PHYS 102/102A), writing (ENGL 101/102), math, and GER, though Years 3 and 4 lean heavily on generic ARCH and free electives (roughly a dozen elective rows), and the final term drops to 12 credits.
dimensions.suggestion: Annotate the gen-ed/architecture-core/elective credit split and firm up the Year 3-4 elective rows (especially the required 'ARCH Elective (Technology)') so the back half of the plan is as concrete as the front half.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: This is the best-presented of the combined degrees: all eight terms are labeled with per-term credits and the plan reconciles. Readability is nonetheless reduced by credit-less GER placeholder rows (Social Sciences GER, Natural Science GER, History and Humanities GER shown with null credits), a wall of generic 'ARCH Elective' rows across Years 3-4, and the absence of any program description or view of the graduate M.S. component.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a short program overview and the graduate-term outline, and assign explicit credits to the null GER rows so every term line carries a value.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The undergraduate architecture content is sound but traditional (studios, history, Construction I-II, the dated CS 104 computing course). For the civil-engineering destination the notable currency gap is engineering fundamentals: there is no statics, mechanics of materials, structural analysis, or calculus in the undergraduate plan, and no visible coverage of building performance/BIM that modern AEC integration expects.
dimensions.suggestion: Add undergraduate statics/mechanics-of-materials and a BIM/structural-modeling course so graduates carry current structural-engineering and digital-delivery skills into the M.S.

descriptionQuality.justification: Most sampled descriptions are present and specific (ARCH 223 names wood/steel/masonry/concrete assemblies; PHYS 102 states its statics-and-dynamics scope; ARCH 210 gives a clear historical range). A few are thin continuation stubs (ARCH 196 'A continuation of ARCH 195...'; ARCH 296 'A continuation ARCH 295...'; ARCH 395 'This course is a continuation of ARCH 296.'), and no graduate civil-engineering course is described.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated clearly and consistently, with sensible grade conditions (ENGL 102 requires ENGL 100/101 with a grade of C or higher; MATH 112 requires MATH 111 with C or better) and correct empty prerequisites on intro courses. The only clarity drag is the operator ambiguity in ARCH 211's 'ARCH 210 or ARCH 251 and ARCH 252' and the handful of graduate/GER danglers.

missingTopics.rationale: A program feeding an M.S. in Civil Engineering shows Construction I-II but no statics or mechanics course, the single most important undergraduate engineering prerequisite missing from the plan.
The undergraduate math is only MATH 107 and MATH 105 (statistics); calculus and differential equations are prerequisites for graduate structural and geotechnical coursework.
No course bridges Construction II (assemblies) to structural behavior of frames and loads, a gap for the civil-engineering track.
HVAC, lighting, and energy performance are absent, yet they are central to integrated architecture-engineering practice.
The only computing course is the dated CS 104; BIM/structural-modeling skills are now standard across the AEC pipeline this degree targets.

emergingTrends.rationale: Modern civil practice is shifting to performance-based methods; the architecture-plus-civil pairing is well placed to teach integrated structural performance once statics/structures are added.
Mass timber and low-carbon concrete are transforming structures; Construction I-II could be extended toward embodied-carbon-aware structural choices.
Model-based structural analysis and digital twins are becoming standard in AEC delivery, arguing for a BIM/structural-modeling course upstream of the M.S.
Flood, seismic, and climate loads increasingly drive civil design, a growth area the combined degree could own.
Parametric and optimization-driven structural design is rising in practice and would modernize the dated CS 104 computing requirement.

topSuggestions: Add the engineering foundation the civil-engineering destination requires: a calculus sequence plus statics/mechanics of materials and an intro structural-analysis course in the undergraduate terms.
Publish explicit program learning outcomes for the integrated architecture-plus-civil-engineering competency (none are currently present).
Modernize the computing/technology requirement from the dated CS 104 toward BIM and structural digital modeling.
Firm up and annotate the Year 3-4 elective load (a dozen generic 'ARCH Elective' rows) and assign credits to the null GER rows so the balanced 120-credit plan reads clearly.
Register the PHYS 102A lab and GER 200/300 categories and link the graduate ARCH 6xx courses so the 9 dangling references resolve.
